questions.question: List the HTTP methods GET, POST, PUT, DELETE and indicate which of them are idempotent.
keyPoints: GET retrieves a representation of a resource; idempotent.
POST submits data to be processed; not idempotent.
PUT replaces a resource at a specific URI; idempotent.

questions.question: Explain the role of TTL (Time To Live) in DNS caching. How does TTL affect the propagation of changes to DNS records?
keyPoints: TTL is a value in seconds specifying how long a DNS resolver may cache a resource record.
A low TTL causes frequent lookups and quicker propagation of changes but increases query load.
A high TTL reduces DNS query traffic but delays propagation of updates.
TTL is set by the authoritative DNS server for each record.

questions.question: Compare HTTP/2 and HTTP/3 with respect to multiplexing, header compression, and security. Also discuss how HTTPS provides confidentiality and integrity, and describe the role of TLS in the handshake process.
keyPoints: HTTP/2 multiplexes multiple streams over a single TCP connection; HTTP/3 uses QUIC (UDP) to eliminate head-of-line blocking.
HTTP/2 uses HPACK for header compression; HTTP/3 uses QPACK, which is more efficient over unreliable QUIC streams.
Both HTTP/2 and HTTP/3 can be run over TLS, but HTTP/3 typically mandates TLS 1.3 and operates over UDP port 443.
HTTPS encrypts the payload using TLS, providing confidentiality, integrity, and server authentication; TLS handshake negotiates cipher suites and exchanges certificates.

questions.question: Explain the categories of HTTP status codes (1xx, 2xx, 3xx, 4xx, 5xx) and give examples of commonly used codes in each category. Additionally, describe how web applications use cookies and session identifiers to maintain state across stateless HTTP requests.
keyPoints: 1xx informational (e.g., 100 Continue), 2xx success (e.g., 200 OK, 201 Created), 3xx redirection (e.g., 301 Moved Permanently, 304 Not Modified), 4xx client error (e.g., 400 Bad Request, 404 Not Found, 401 Unauthorized), 5xx server error (e.g., 500 Internal Server Error, 503 Service Unavailable).
Cookies are small pieces of data stored on the client and sent with each HTTP request; the server issues a Set-Cookie header to set them.
Session identifiers (session IDs) are stored in cookies or URL parameters; the server maps the ID to session data stored server-side, enabling stateful behavior.
Secure flags (HttpOnly, Secure) and SameSite attributes help mitigate XSS and CSRF attacks.
